label: Official Direct Deaths
contestNote: Official Soviet figure covers only acute radiation syndrome deaths within weeks of accident. Independent estimates range from 4,000 to 985,000 long-term cancer deaths.

label: Liquidators Deployed
deltaNote: Emergency workers deployed in cleanup, decontamination, and construction operations

label: People Evacuated & Resettled
deltaNote: Including Pripyat and 30 km zone residents; later expanded resettlements from contaminated areas in Belarus, Ukraine, Russia

label: Exclusion Zone Area
source: Ukrainian government; State Agency of Ukraine on Exclusion Zone Management
deltaNote: The Chornobyl Exclusion Zone remains the largest radiation exclusion zone in the world

label: Childhood Thyroid Cancer Cases
source: UNSCEAR 2018; WHO; Journal of Thyroid Research
deltaNote: Cases diagnosed 1991–2015 in persons who were under 18 at time of accident (Belarus, Russia, Ukraine); ~6,000 cases attributed to Chernobyl by 2005; UNSCEAR 2018 revised to 20,000+ across full age range; most are treatable with surgery

label: Estimated Cancer Deaths (UNSCEAR/WHO)
contestNote: Conservative UNSCEAR/WHO estimate for most highly exposed populations. Greenpeace (2006) estimates 985,000. TORCH Report estimates 30,000–60,000 in Europe.

label: New Safe Confinement Cost
source: EBRD — New Safe Confinement Project Final Cost
deltaNote: The New Safe Confinement arch completed in 2016 is the largest movable structure ever built

label: Years Since Explosion
source: Reactor No. 4 exploded 26 April 1986 at 01:23 local time
deltaNote: As of 26 April 2026 — the 40th anniversary. The NSC is designed to contain the reactor for at least another 100 years.
